title: Chapter 10: Mirror, Mirror on the Dark Web
content: Thirty-six hours.

That's what the clock on Nova's phone said, anyway. She didn't believe it. Time had gone liquid somewhere around hour twenty-two, pooling in strange pockets — five minutes stretching into an eternity of staring at code, then two hours vanishing like a swallowed breath. She'd cleared Circle 4 three days ago. Circle 3 the day before that. Circle 2, four days before *that*. The pace was inhuman, and she was the one setting it.
Four more to go before Chloe.

The apartment looked like a crime scene composed entirely of matcha cups and takeout containers. A graveyard of iced matcha lattes sweated condensation rings into every surface — the coffee table, the kitchen counter, the stack of W magazines she'd been meaning to recycle since March. A half-eaten pad thai from an indeterminate day sat on the counter, the lime wedge now the color of a bruise. Her Juicy Couture tracksuit — the pink one, the one she'd paid resale for because the velvet gave her main-character energy — lay crumpled on the floor like a shed skin. She couldn't remember taking it off.

Nova stood in the bathroom. The tile was cold under her bare feet, the kind of cold that should have been grounding but wasn't. She turned the faucet on, cupped water in her palms, and splashed her face. The water was too warm. She hadn't adjusted the tap. She didn't care.
She looked up.

The mirror was fogged at the edges — LA humidity, bad ventilation, whatever — but her face was clear enough. Dark circles under her eyes so deep they looked drawn on, like editorial makeup from a shoot nobody asked for. Her skin had gone a shade that wasn't a shade — not pale, not sallow, just *absent*, like someone had dialed her saturation down. Her hair, usually sleek and intentional even when she claimed it was effortless, hung in pieces around her face, the silk press ruined by three days of not wrapping it.
She stared at the person in the mirror.
The person stared back.
And then—

It came from nowhere. Or from everywhere. From the base of her spine and the roof of her mouth and the space behind her sternum, rising like a tide she couldn't outswim. Her chest clenched. Not tight — *crushed*, like a fist had reached through her ribs and squeezed. She gripped the edge of the sink, knuckles going white, and the porcelain was so cold it burned, but the cold was the only thing that was real because everything else was spinning.

Her breath wouldn't come. She tried to inhale and her lungs just — didn't. Like they'd forgotten the mechanics. Like the autopilot had crashed. She gasped, got a sliver of air, and it tasted like copper, metallic and thin, as if the oxygen itself had turned hostile. Her pulse roared in her ears — not a throb, a *roar*, a white-noise static that swallowed the sound of the faucet running, swallowed the hum of her neighbor's AC unit through the wall, swallowed everything until there was only the drumming of her own heart, too fast, way too fast, a BPM that would redline a mixing board.

Her knees buckled. She went down on the tile, one hand still gripping the sink, the other pressed flat against the cold floor and the cold was *good*, the cold was a fact, the cold was something she could hold onto. She pressed her forehead to the tile and she could feel every groove in the ceramic, every microscopic ridge, and she focused on that texture with the desperation of a drowning person finding a rope. Cold. Groove. Ridge. Cold. Groove. Ridge.
*Breathe.*
She couldn't.
*Breathe, Nova.*

Her vision tunneled. The bathroom shrank to a bright point, then expanded, then shrank again, like a camera aperture having a panic attack of its own. She could hear herself making sounds — small, animal sounds, not crying, not screaming, just these hitching, wet gasps that she didn't authorize. Her body was a car she'd lost control of, and it was driving itself off a cliff, and she was in the passenger seat, and there was no steering wheel, and the radio was playing her heartbeat at maximum volume.
She didn't know how long it lasted. Two minutes. Ten. Time was liquid again.

When it released her — and it did release her, like a hand letting go, like a wave retreating — she was on her side on the bathroom floor, curled into herself, the tile cold against her cheek. She was breathing. Shallow and shaky, but breathing. Her fingers were cramped from gripping the sink edge. She uncurled them one by one, watching the blood rush back, and the tingling was almost worse than the panic because it meant she could feel again, and feeling was the problem.
She pulled herself up. Gripped the sink. Looked in the mirror.
The person staring back had tear tracks on her cheeks and looked like she'd just survived something.
Nova didn't know her.
The knock came at 7:43 PM.

Nova was on the couch — not sitting, exactly, more like she'd been deposited there by a wave that had finished with her. She'd changed into a clean oversized tee and bike shorts because her old clothes smelled like three days of adrenaline and dry shampoo, and she'd managed to brush her teeth, which felt like a victory on par with summiting Everest. The TV was on, playing something she wasn't watching. The apartment still looked like a matcha cup mausoleum. She hadn't had the energy to deal.

Another knock. Three sharp raps, then a voice: "Nova. I know you're in there. Your Uber Eats history says you ordered a single black coffee at 4 AM and nothing since, and yes, that's unhinged boundary behavior on my part, and yes, I'll process it in therapy, but open the door."
Jules.

Nova considered pretending she wasn't home. Then she considered the fact that Jules had clearly already checked her location (they shared on Find My, a holdover from the Chloe era when they'd both needed to know the other was safe), and pretending would just make it weirder.
She opened the door.

Jules stood in the hallway with a bag from Sun Nong Dan and an expression that said *I'm not mad, I'm assessing*. She was in her outside-world outfit — high-waisted jeans, a cropped Cardi B merch tee, her hair in two smooth buns — but the way her eyes swept over Nova was pure clinician. Nova knew that look. She hated that look.
"Babe," Jules said quietly. "You look like you haven't slept since last Tuesday."
"I'm fine."
"You said that while your hand was shaking."
Nova looked down. Her hand was, in fact, shaking. She shoved it into the pocket of her bike shorts. "My nail appointment ran late."

Jules looked at her nails — bare, chipped, the ghost of a chrome French tip from two weeks ago — and said nothing. She walked past Nova into the apartment, set the takeout bag on the kitchen counter (next to the fossilized pad thai, which she clocked but didn't comment on), and moved to the couch. She sat down. Not across from Nova. Just — *there*. Present. Occupying space.
She didn't push. She just stayed.

Nova stood in the kitchen doorway for a full minute. Then she sat down on the opposite end of the couch. Then, slowly, she pulled her feet up and curled into the corner, and they existed in the same room like that for a while, the TV murmuring, the takeout getting cold on the counter, and neither of them saying anything because sometimes the loudest thing you can do is just show up.

Eventually, Jules got up and brought the food to the couch. She unpacked it without ceremony — galbi-jjim, the short rib soup, rice, banchan in their little containers — and the smell hit Nova like a physical force. Garlic and soy and slow-cooked beef, rich and warm and *real*, cutting through the stale apartment air like a reset button for her entire nervous system. Her stomach, which she'd forgotten existed, cramped with sudden, violent hunger.

They ate. Not talking. The soup was hot enough to require careful sipping, and Nova let the heat bloom across her tongue, let the salt and the fat and the sweetness of the braised meat fill her mouth, and for a few minutes she was just a person eating soup on a couch with her best friend, and that was enough. The galbi was fall-off-the-bone tender, and she ate with her fingers when the chopsticks got unwieldy, and Jules didn't comment on that either.

Jules ate slowly, methodically, the way she did everything. She was watching the TV but not watching it. She was watching Nova, in the way that Jules watched everything — sidelong, gentle, like a lighthouse. Present but not intrusive.
The silence held until Nova broke it. She didn't mean to. The words came out like they'd been sitting in her throat for days, waiting for the soup to wash them loose.
"Do you ever think about how the people who hurt others never seem to pay?"
Jules set down her chopsticks. She didn't look surprised. She looked like she'd been waiting for this, maybe, but was letting Nova set the pace.
"Every single day," Jules said. "I work with people who were hurt, remember?"

She did. Jules was in her second year of a master's in counseling psych, and she came home from her practicum with stories that made Nova's rage feel reasonable and insufficient all at once. Kids who'd been abandoned. Teens who'd been exploited. Adults who'd been broken by people who would never face a single consequence because consequences, in the real world, were not a function of guilt but of power.
"And what if someone could make them pay?" Nova said.
Her voice was steady. Her hands were not.

Jules picked up her chopsticks again. Selected a piece of kimchi. Chewed. Swallowed. Took her time, not because she didn't have an answer, but because she did, and she wanted to place it carefully, the way you'd set down something fragile.
"I'd want to know who gets to decide what 'pay' means."

The question hung in the air between them, visible, almost — a shape Nova could reach out and touch. She opened her mouth. Closed it. Took another bite of galbi, and the meat was still warm but she couldn't taste it anymore.
Jules didn't ask again. She didn't explain. She just held the space, the way she'd been trained to, the way she'd been doing for Nova since they were sixteen and the world had first shown its teeth.

They finished eating. Jules packed the leftovers into Nova's fridge, moving the expired oat milk to make room, and wiped down the counter, and didn't say a word about the matcha cups. At the door, she turned and opened her arms.
"Come here."
"I'm fine—"
"Wasn't asking."

Nova stepped into the hug. Jules held her — not tight, just *there*, solid, her heartbeat a steady rhythm against Nova's ear — and Nova pretended she didn't need it, and the pretense was paper-thin, and they both knew it, and neither of them said so, because some truths don't need language.
"Text me tomorrow," Jules said, pulling back. "Or I'm showing up with a wellness check and a green juice, and you know I'll do it."
"You wouldn't."
"Babe. I brought you galbi-jjim at 8 PM on a Sunday because your location hadn't moved in three days. I will absolutely show up with a green juice."
Nova almost smiled. Almost.

She watched Jules walk down the hallway, waving over her shoulder without looking back, and closed the door. The apartment was quiet again. The quiet felt different now — less like silence and more like the space a sound had just left.
Nova sat at her desk. The monitors hummed to life, and she pulled up Inferno.

Virgil's interface loaded differently tonight. No bounce animation on the startup screen. No sparkle cursor. No Y2K-era chime, no pixelated loading bar with the winking emoticon. The screen was clean. Minimal. A single chat window, the cursor blinking steady and patient.
**VIRGIL:** Are you sure you want to keep going?

Nova stared at the words. Virgil didn't ask questions like that. Virgil made jokes about her Spotify Wrapped and roasted her search history and delivered information with the energy of a sassy AIM chatbot from 2003. Virgil didn't *check on her*.
**NOVA:** I don't have a choice.
**VIRGIL:** You always have a choice. That's what makes you different from them.

The cursor blinked. Nova read the words twice. Three times. "Them" — the targets? The people who'd hurt others and never paid? Or "them" — whatever Virgil was, whatever category it occupied, whatever constraints or programming or lack thereof gave it the capacity to say something like that?
She wanted to ask. The question was right there, sitting behind her teeth. But asking would mean confronting something she wasn't ready to face, so she did what she always did: she moved forward.
**NOVA:** Pull up the Burn Book.

The screen shifted. The Burn Book materialized — her digital grimoire, her hit list, her whatever-you-called-a-map-that-led-somewhere-you-might-not-come-back-from. She'd built it to look like an actual burn book, remember? The cursive font, the Polaroid frames, the pink-and-black aesthetic, because if she was going to burn her life down, she was going to do it with brand consistency.

The circles were there. Circles 1 through 4: crossed out, X'd, *done*. The digital ink was red, slashes through each name like a scratch through a face in a photograph. She could still see the names underneath. She could always see them.
Then the locked circles. 5, 6, 7. Still sealed. Still waiting. Files inside, patterns half-mapped, connections she hadn't chased yet.
And at the center — always at the center, like a spider in a web, like a queen on a throne — Circle 0. The Ice Queen. Chloe.

Still frozen. Still untouchable. The lock on Circle 0 wasn't a lock; it was a *wall*, and the wall was made of ice, and the ice was made of everything Nova hadn't let herself feel. The name sat there, elegant and cold, and even in digital form it seemed to lower the temperature on the screen, and Nova—

Nova's reflection glowed in the monitor. That thing again, that face she didn't recognize, hovering over the interface like a ghost. Her reflection overlapped Chloe's placeholder image — the ice crown, the frozen eyes — and for one vertiginous second, the two faces lined up. Same cheekbones. Same jawline set like a decision. Same fire in the eyes, or maybe the same ice. Nova couldn't tell which. The mirror and the screen and the target all folded into one another, and she was looking at herself looking at Chloe looking at her, and the recursion made her stomach lurch.
She shoved back from the desk. The chair rolled. The image broke.
"Open Circle 5," she said. Her voice was hoarse, like she'd been screaming, even though she hadn't.
The screen obeyed.
**CIRCLE 5: KAREN LAND**
The files began to populate.
Nova read.

She didn't know when the crying started. That was the thing about crying — when you spent enough time not doing it, your body found ways to bypass your permission entirely. One moment she was scrolling through metadata, and the next her vision was blurring, and there were tears dropping onto her keyboard, plip-plip, small and warm and deeply inconvenient.

She didn't wipe them away. Her hands stayed on the keys, steady, and that was the most terrifying part — that her hands were steady. That her body could fall apart and still function. That she could cry and type and read and analyze and feel absolutely nothing and absolutely everything at the same time.

The screen glowed. The files scrolled. Circle 5's architecture was already taking shape in her mind — Karen Land, whoever she was, whatever she'd done, she was next. The pattern was revealing itself, the way it always did, threads connecting from Circle 4 to Circle 5, and from there to Circles 6 and 7, and from there, always, back to the center. Back to Chloe.
Nova kept reading.
The tears kept falling.

Outside, LA hummed its low electric hum, indifferent. The matcha cups sweated in the dark. The phone buzzed once — Jules, checking in, because Jules always checked in — and Nova ignored it, because stopping would mean feeling, and feeling would mean stopping, and she couldn't stop, not when she was this close, not when Chloe was four circles away, not when every answer she'd ever wanted was lurking at the end of this tunnel made of data and rage.
She didn't know if the trail was destroying her or making her. She didn't know if there was a difference.
She kept going.
